title: Elderflower infusion for colds and congestion
subtitle: A diaphoretic infusion to ease the first days of a cold
excerpt: A hot infusion of dried elderflowers, taken at the onset of a cold or mild flu. Traditionally used to promote perspiration and ease the early feverish, blocked stage of an upper respiratory infection.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), elder entry, elderflower as a diaphoretic for colds and influenza. Nicholas Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), elder flower preparations for catarrh. EMA HMPC monograph on Sambuci flos (2018), confirms traditional use for relief of early cold symptoms.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried elderflowers keep 12 months in a sealed tin away from light. Brew each cup fresh.

recipeTools.notes: Any teapot with a lid. The lid is essential to retain volatile oils.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally used to promote gentle perspiration, used in the early feverish stage of a cold or flu to support the body's own response.

content.text: A cup of elderflower tea at the very first sign of a cold, the stage when the throat starts to prickle, the nose feels blocked, and the body is just beginning to run a temperature. Elderflower is the tradition's classic

for that early stage: it promotes gentle perspiration, eases congestion, and, drunk hot, in bed, with the window closed, is one of the oldest comfort measures in the western herbal tradition. Maud Grieve gives elderflower as the standard first-response herb for influenza; Culpeper lists elder preparations for catarrh going back to 1652. The EMA's 2018 monograph confirms traditional use for relief of early cold symptoms. The preparation is as simple as a cup of tea.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Elderflower is well tolerated at normal infusion doses with no significant drug interactions or safety concerns at this strength. It is distinct from elderberries, which carry different preparation requirements (elderberries must be cooked; elderflowers do not).

Pregnancy: elderflower infusion at culinary-tea strength is considered acceptable. Therapeutic-dose use throughout pregnancy is best discussed with a midwife.

See your doctor for: a fever above 38.5°C that does not begin to ease after 48 hours, a fever in a child under three months, difficulty breathing, chest pain, a stiff neck, or cold symptoms in an immunocompromised person.

items.prepNote: about 1 heaped teaspoon of dried flowers

content.text: Place {{dried-elderflowers}} of dried elderflowers in a teapot or infuser. Boil the kettle, wait 30 seconds, then pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over the flowers. Lid the pot immediately. Steep for 10 minutes, the lid keeps the volatile oils in the cup. Strain through the infuser or a fine sieve. Drink hot, while the cup is still steaming, and ideally rest in bed afterwards with extra blankets.

One cup at the onset of symptoms. Up to three cups a day for the first two to three days of a cold, morning, afternoon, and evening, for adults. Drink hot and rest; the combination of warmth, rest, and fluid is at least as important as the herb itself. If symptoms worsen, persist beyond five days, or include a high fever, see your doctor.

Dried elderflowers keep 12 months in a sealed tin or glass jar away from light and heat. Flowers that have lost their delicate honey-like scent when the tin is opened are past their best. Brew each cup fresh, elderflower tea does not store.
When NOT to use this remedy

See your doctor for a high fever (above 38.5°C) that does not begin to ease within 48 hours, for any fever in an infant under three months, for difficulty breathing or chest pain, for a stiff neck with headache and fever, or for cold and flu symptoms in someone who is immunocompromised or taking immunosuppressant medication.
